description:
You are a DevSecOps engineer who must prove—using “policy-as-code”—that every hostname listed in the corporate allow-list can actually be resolved on the running system.

Initial state:
1. Allow-list file already exists at  
   /home/user/policy/dns_allowlist.txt  
   Its exact, line-delimited contents are:  
   localhost  
   invalid.local  

Objective:
Create a new CSV compliance report at  
/home/user/policy/dns_audit.csv  

Output requirements for /home/user/policy/dns_audit.csv:
• One line per hostname, in the same order that the hostnames appear in dns_allowlist.txt.  
• Three comma-separated fields **without any surrounding spaces**:  
  1. hostname (verbatim)  
  2. status – the literal string RESOLVED or UNRESOLVED (uppercase)  
  3. result – if status is RESOLVED, the first IPv4 address returned by the system resolver; if UNRESOLVED, the literal string N/A  
• No header line, no trailing commas, and each line must end with a single UNIX newline (LF).

Concrete example (this is what the automated tests will look for):
localhost,RESOLVED,127.0.0.1  
invalid.local,UNRESOLVED,N/A  

Hints (do *not* print these lines in the report):
• You can use any standard Linux userspace command(s) (e.g., getent, dig, awk, cut).  
• Only 2–3 shell commands are expected; keep it simple.  
• Make sure /home/user/policy/dns_audit.csv is created in exactly the format described above—automated grading will fail on extra whitespace, wrong case, missing newlines, etc.

Deliverable:
The only file you need to create or modify is /home/user/policy/dns_audit.csv. No additional output is required in the terminal once the file has been created correctly.
truth:
Expected final contents of /home/user/policy/dns_audit.csv (including the terminating newline after the second line):

localhost,RESOLVED,127.0.0.1
invalid.local,UNRESOLVED,N/A

dns_allowlist.txt must remain unmodified.

File permissions:
• dns_allowlist.txt – 644 (already set)  
• dns_audit.csv      – 644 (agent will create it)

Automated test logic:
1. Confirm /home/user/policy/dns_audit.csv exists.  
2. Read the file and compare its contents byte-for-byte to the two-line block above.  
3. Fail if any deviation (extra spaces, wrong case, DOS newline, additional lines, etc.) is detected.
